articles.title: Meet Mi: The Monster Who Wants to Sleep
articles.summary: The philosophy behind Migraine Companion — why treating migraine as a companion (not enemy) works better. Mi's 5 mood states reflect self-care behaviors. Backed by narrative therapy and research on hydration, breathing, and self-compassion.
key_facts: Michael White's externalization technique: 'The person is not the problem'
Increasing water by 1.5L reduced headache hours by 21 over two weeks
4-7-8 breathing activates parasympathetic nervous system
Self-compassion reduces pain catastrophizing and improves outcomes
Mi has 5 mood states: Sleeping, Calm, Restless, Agitated, Upset

articles.title: Your First Neurologist Visit: What to Bring, What to Ask
articles.summary: How to prepare for and maximize a neurologist appointment. What to bring (diary data, medication history, family history), what questions to ask, and how Migraine Companion reports help communicate your story.
key_facts: Average neurology appointment is 45 minutes or less
Headache diary data is the single most useful diagnostic tool
Key questions: frequency, severity, triggers, family history, prior treatments
Migraine Companion generates shareable reports for doctors

articles.title: I Am Not My Migraine: Separating Self from Pain
articles.summary: How chronic illness fuses with identity and how narrative therapy's externalization technique helps separate the person from the condition. Identity fusion is learned and can be unlearned.
key_facts: Chronic illness can become fused with identity — 'I am a migraineur' vs 'I experience migraines'
Narrative therapy externalization reduces self-blame
Identity fusion increases pain catastrophizing and disability
Separation improves sense of agency and control

articles.title: The Words We Use: How Language Shapes Your Migraine Experience
articles.summary: How language shapes illness experience. 'Suffering from,' 'battling,' 'victim of' create combat framing that increases suffering. Rewriting your migraine story changes how you feel about it.
key_facts: Combat metaphors increase helplessness and catastrophizing
Language shapes neural pain processing pathways
27 peer-reviewed references including Neurology (n=59,001)
Reframing language improves self-efficacy and perceived control

articles.title: Why We Don't Fight Migraines: The Science of Companionization
articles.summary: The science behind companionization vs combat framing. Framing chronic illness as a battle increases suffering. Companion-based approach grounded in psychological research.
key_facts: Combat framing increases stress hormones and pain sensitivity
Companionization reduces cortisol and sympathetic activation
Narrative therapy techniques reduce chronic pain disability
Self-compassion correlates with lower migraine frequency

articles.title: From 20 Days to 2: Stories of Successful Management
articles.summary: Real stories of people who dramatically reduced migraine days through combination approaches. 22 peer-reviewed references. Shows that improvement is possible but takes time and persistence.
key_facts: Combination approaches work better than single interventions
Improvement often takes months to years of experimentation
Lifestyle modifications amplify medication effectiveness
Tracking patterns is essential for finding personal triggers

articles.title: I Built a Migraine App During 30 Years of Pain (and a War)
articles.summary: Rustam's personal story: from first attack at age 12 to building Migraine Companion during wartime in Ukraine. From 15-20 attacks/month to 3-4 through self-management.
key_facts: First migraine at age 12 with visual aura
Reduced from 15-20 attacks/month to 3-4
Built app during active wartime in Ukraine
Narrative therapy principles transformed approach to illness

articles.title: The Conference Room Confession: Migraine at Work
articles.summary: Navigating migraine in professional settings. Stigma, hiding, accommodation requests, and the courage to stop pretending. Based on Goffman's stigma theory.
key_facts: Workplace stigma causes most migraineurs to hide their condition
Presenteeism costs exceed absenteeism costs for employers
ADA may provide workplace accommodations for chronic migraine
Disclosure decisions depend on workplace culture and manager relationship

articles.title: The Third Person in Every Room: How Migraine Lives in Your Relationships
articles.summary: How migraine affects partnerships, family, and friendships. 72% of chronic migraineurs feel they'd be better partners without their condition. Communication strategies and partner support.
key_facts: 72% feel they would be better partners without migraine
71.7% believe family doesn't understand their condition
Cancelled plans and darkened bedrooms strain relationships
Partner education and communication protocols improve outcomes

articles.title: You Don't Look Sick: The Loneliness of Invisible Pain
articles.summary: The stigma of invisible illness. 'But you seem fine' — five words that cut deeper than the migraine itself. Validation, isolation, and finding people who understand.
key_facts: Migraine is an invisible disability — no external markers
Stigma leads to delayed diagnosis and undertreated pain
Social isolation worsens migraine outcomes
Peer support communities improve coping and reduce isolation

articles.title: Beyond CGRP: The PACAP Revolution and the Future of Migraine Treatment
articles.summary: PACAP is a migraine-triggering neuropeptide independent of CGRP. 40-70% don't respond to anti-CGRP drugs — PACAP may be why. First drugs targeting PACAP itself (not PAC1 receptor) show promise.
key_facts: PACAP operates completely independently of CGRP pathway
40-70% of people don't get adequate relief from anti-CGRP therapies
2025 RCT proved blocking CGRP doesn't prevent PACAP-triggered migraines
First PAC1 receptor drug (AMG 301) failed; targeting PACAP molecule itself is the breakthrough
Phase 2 trials show promise for anti-PACAP antibodies

articles.title: Beyond the Pill: What Science Actually Says About Magnesium, Riboflavin, and CoQ10
articles.summary: Evidence-based review of top 3 supplements. Magnesium 400-600mg (AMF recommended), riboflavin 400mg (59% responder rate), CoQ10 300mg. What forms, doses, and timelines to expect.
key_facts: Magnesium citrate 600mg: significant migraine frequency reduction
Riboflavin 400mg: 59% had ≥50% reduction in attacks (NNT=2.3)
CoQ10 300mg: reduced attacks from 4.9 to 3.2/month
AMF recommends magnesium 400-600mg for prevention
Start low, build over 2 weeks, give 3 months to see results

articles.title: Blaming the Barometer: The Weather-Migraine Myth
articles.summary: 50-80% believe weather triggers attacks but only ~20% have reliable connection. The 'bucket theory' explains inconsistency: cumulative load matters, not single factors. Prodrome may increase weather sensitivity.
key_facts: 50-80% believe weather triggers attacks
Only ~20% show reliable weather-migraine connection in studies
Bucket theory: migraine fires when cumulative triggers exceed threshold
Prodrome symptoms starting 24-48h before may increase weather sensitivity
Controllable factors matter more than weather

articles.title: 31 Needles of Hope: The Truth About Botox for Migraine
articles.summary: Botox blocks pain-signaling molecules (CGRP, substance P, glutamate) from trigeminal nerve endings. PREEMPT trials (n=1,384). 31 injections every 12 weeks. FDA-approved only for chronic migraine (≥15 days/month).
key_facts: Works by blocking CGRP/substance P/glutamate from trigeminal nerve endings
PREEMPT trials (n=1,384): significant reduction in headache days
31 injections across 7 head/neck areas every 12 weeks
Full benefit typically requires 2-3 cycles (6-9 months)
Only for chronic migraine (≥15 days/month), not episodic
Side effects mild: neck pain 9%, eyelid droop 4%

articles.title: The Anxious Brain: The Bidirectional Link Between Migraine and Anxiety
articles.summary: Migraine and anxiety share bidirectional relationship and common neurobiology: serotonin, CGRP in amygdala, HPA axis. Interictal burden (anxiety between attacks) may cause more disability than attacks themselves.
key_facts: Bidirectional: each increases risk of the other
Share serotonin dysfunction, CGRP activity, HPA axis dysregulation
CGRP causes anxiety by depleting dopamine in hippocampus
Interictal anxiety may drive more disability than attacks
CGRP treatments can improve both conditions simultaneously
CBT dismantles fear-avoidance cycle

articles.title: The Hormone Rollercoaster: Menstrual Migraines Explained
articles.summary: Menstrual migraine in 5-day window (day -2 to +3). Trigger is speed of estrogen decline. Three cascades: serotonin crash + opioid shutdown + prostaglandin surge → CGRP activation.
key_facts: Menstrual migraine: 5-day window day -2 to +3 of period
Migraineurs show 46% steeper estrogen drops (SWAN, n=3,302)
Frovatriptan 2.5mg reduces menstrual migraine from 67% to 41%
CGRP antibodies cut menstrual migraine days from 5 to 2 per cycle
3 months of dual diary tracking is diagnostic foundation

articles.title: The CGRP Revolution: The Molecule That Changed Everything
articles.summary: CGRP discovery and 8 FDA-approved therapies. STRIVE trial: 50% on erenumab had migraine days cut by half. HER-MES: erenumab better tolerated and more effective than topiramate. AHS recommends as first-line 2024.
key_facts: CGRP discovered 1982-83, linked to migraine in 1990s
8 FDA-approved CGRP therapies: 4 antibodies + 4 gepants
STRIVE (n=955): 50% on erenumab 140mg had ≥50% reduction
HER-MES: erenumab beat topiramate, 4x fewer dropouts
AHS made CGRP first-line prevention in 2024
5-year safety data shows sustained benefits

articles.title: The Exercise Paradox: When Movement Is Both the Trigger and the Cure
articles.summary: Exercise can trigger or prevent migraine. Unstructured sudden-intensity exercise on dehydrated/tired body = trigger. Regular moderate planned exercise matches efficacy of preventive drugs.
key_facts: Regular moderate exercise matches efficacy of preventive medications
Sudden intense exercise on dehydrated/tired body triggers attacks
Gradual progression prevents exercise-triggered migraine
Same activity done differently produces opposite results
Start low, go slow, build habit

articles.title: The Forbidden Molecule: Psilocybin, Psychedelics, and the Future of Migraine
articles.summary: Psilocybin is structurally related to serotonin, binds same receptors as triptans. Yale trial: single dose reduced attacks for 2 weeks. 75% relief in Canadian survey. All trials small (n=10-18). Legal barriers.
key_facts: Psilocin binds 5-HT1B/1D receptors (same as triptans)
Yale trial: single dose reduced migraine days by 1.65/week for 2 weeks
75% reported headache relief in 2025 Canadian survey
Single dose increases dendritic spine density ~10% within 24 hours
NEVER combine with triptans or SSRIs/SNRIs (serotonin syndrome risk)
All trials small (n=10-18) — not evidence-based treatment yet

articles.title: The Gepant Generation: Nurtec, Ubrelvy, Qulipta — A Real-World Guide
articles.summary: First oral migraine-specific drugs. Nurtec: acute + preventive. Ubrelvy: acute only. Qulipta: prevention only. Pain freedom at 2h ~20%, relief ~60%. No cardiovascular risk unlike triptans.
key_facts: First oral drugs designed specifically for migraine
No cardiovascular risks (unlike triptans)
Nurtec: both acute and preventive use
Ubrelvy: acute only, allows redosing at 2 hours
Qulipta: prevention only, strong constipation signal in real-world data
If one gepant fails, another might work — different pharmacokinetics

articles.title: The Hyperexcitable Brain: Why Migraineurs Are Wired Differently
articles.summary: Migraine brain is cortically hyperexcitable — neurons fire more easily. Glutamate/GABA imbalance. Habituation deficit (failure to tune out stimuli). 123 risk loci identified. May have been evolutionary advantage.
key_facts: Neurons fire more easily and intensely, even between attacks
Glutamate/GABA imbalance tips toward overactivation
Habituation deficit: most consistent finding in migraine research
Thalamocortical dysrhythmia floods cortex with sensory info
123 risk loci, 40-60% heritability
Hyperexcitable brain may have enhanced survival in evolution

articles.title: The Ozempic Effect: Why Weight Loss Drugs Are Reducing Migraines
articles.summary: GLP-1 drugs (semaglutide, liraglutide) showing migraine reduction. 2025 pilot: liraglutide cut chronic migraine days nearly in half. Three pathways: intracranial pressure, CGRP suppression, metabolic inflammation reversal.
key_facts: 2025 pilot: liraglutide cut chronic migraine days ~50%
Weight loss didn't explain improvement
GLP-1 may lower intracranial pressure, suppress CGRP, reverse metabolic inflammation
Largest study (n≈11,000/group): fewer ER visits on GLP-1
Obesity raises chronic migraine risk by up to 75%
NOT approved for migraine — no large RCT completed
solutions: discuss with neurologist if on GLP-1

articles.title: The Second Brain: How Your Gut Talks to Your Migraine
articles.summary: 90% of serotonin is made in the gut. Gut-brain axis connects microbiome to migraine. Gut bacteria composition differs in migraineurs. Food tracking matters because of this connection.
key_facts: 90% of serotonin produced in gut, not brain
Gut microbiome composition differs in migraineurs
Gut-brain axis communicates via vagus nerve
Inflammation in gut can trigger neuroinflammation
Probiotics may reduce migraine frequency (emerging research)

articles.title: The Storm Before the Storm: How Mi Predicts Your Migraines
articles.summary: Migraine begins up to 48 hours before pain. Mi tracks 40+ factors to detect prodrome patterns. Food triggers vary individually — Mi learns YOUR patterns, not population averages.
key_facts: Attack begins 24-48 hours before pain
40+ factors tracked for pattern detection
Individual triggers differ from population averages
Skipped meals among most underestimated triggers
Pattern recognition improves with more data over weeks/months

articles.title: Why Your Brain Hates Mondays: Weekend Migraines Explained
articles.summary: Stress decline (not stress itself) triggers let-down migraines. Risk peaks in first 6 hours of relaxation. Cortisol withdrawal + sleep schedule change + caffeine timing shift = trigger stacking.
key_facts: Stress DECLINE is the trigger, not stress itself
Risk peaks in first 6 hours after relaxation (5x increase)
Cortisol withdrawal removes natural anti-inflammatory protection
Triple stacking: sleep schedule + caffeine timing + meal shifts
Hypothalamus craves consistency — even positive changes are threats

articles.title: Zapping the Pain Away: The Truth About Neuromodulation Devices
articles.summary: 6 FDA-cleared devices: Cefaly, gammaCore, SAVI Dual, Nerivio, SpringTMS, sTMS mini. 2025 IHS guidelines recommend several. 50-60% report meaningful benefit. Drug-free option.
key_facts: 6 FDA-cleared non-invasive devices for migraine
2025 IHS guidelines recommend Cefaly, Nerivio, SAVI Dual for acute treatment
50-60% of users report meaningful benefit
Nerivio: broadest evidence, most insurance coverage, lowest cost
SAVI Dual: best data for migraine with aura
Best as part of combination approach, not standalone

articles.title: Ancient Needles, Modern Evidence: Acupuncture for Migraine
articles.summary: Cochrane evidence supports acupuncture as preventive. JAMA trial: 67% reduction, lasting months after treatment. May reduce CGRP. NICE recommends when meds fail. 6-10 sessions over 5-8 weeks.
key_facts: Cochrane moderate-quality evidence as effective preventive
JAMA trial: 67% reduction in migraine frequency
Benefits last months after treatment ends
May reduce CGRP (same target as modern drugs)
NICE recommends when standard meds fail
Plan 6-10 sessions over 5-8 weeks, 3x/week initially

articles.title: Flying with a Ticking Clock: How to Survive Air Travel with Migraine
articles.summary: Cabin hypoxia triggers migraine in up to 63%. Descent is most dangerous phase. Early medication timing: 84% pain-free at 2h if treated early vs 53% when pain escalated. Pre/in/post-flight protocols.
key_facts: Low-oxygen conditions trigger migraine in up to 63% of susceptible people
Descent is most dangerous: rapid pressure changes activate trigeminal nerve
Early treatment: 84% pain-free at 2h vs 53% when pain escalated
All prescription drugs allowed through TSA in carry-on
Post-landing: cortisol drop + circadian disruption + hypoxia recovery

articles.title: Learning to Control the Uncontrollable: Biofeedback for Migraine
articles.summary: Grade A evidence for migraine prevention, on par with propranolol. Three types: thermal, EMG, HRV. Matches medication in head-to-head trials. Effects last: stable at 17 months, only 9% relapse vs 39% for propranolol.
key_facts: Grade A evidence — on par with propranolol and topiramate
Three types: thermal, EMG, and HRV biofeedback
Combined with medication: 79% improvement vs 54% alone
Stable at 17 months; 9% relapse vs 39% for propranolol
Home practice 10 min/day is #1 predictor of success
Only 18% of referred patients complete even one session — access is bottleneck

articles.title: Lighting the Way: Creating a Migraine-Friendly Home
articles.summary: Eyes contain cells (ipRGCs) sending pain signals directly to brain. Green 530nm light reduces pain ~20%. LED flicker triggers 41% of migraineurs. Warm light ≤2700K, flicker-free drivers recommended.
key_facts: ipRGCs send pain signals to brain separate from vision
Blue, amber, red light activate pain pathway most
Green light 530nm reduces pain by ~20%
Green light therapy reduced headache days by ~60%
41% developed headaches from standard LED flicker vs 8% controls
Chronic darkness worsens photophobia over time

articles.title: The 4-7-8 Breathing Technique for Migraine Relief
articles.summary: 4-7-8 breathing activates parasympathetic nervous system. 2022 RCT: diaphragmatic breathing reduced migraine frequency. AMF confirms paced breathing reduces attacks.
key_facts: Inhale 4, hold 7, exhale 8 activates parasympathetic system
2022 RCT: significant migraine frequency reduction
Heart rate slows, blood pressure drops, muscle tension releases
AMF confirms paced breathing reduces attack frequency
Breathing Garden in app uses this technique

articles.title: The Vagus Nerve Hack: Simple Techniques That Calm Your Migraine Brain
articles.summary: Vagus nerve is longest cranial nerve. Activating it dials down migraine sensitivity. Techniques: cold water, slow exhale, humming. Can work in minutes.
key_facts: Vagus nerve is longest cranial nerve — connects brain to gut
Activation shifts from sympathetic to parasympathetic dominance
Cold exposure activates dive reflex and vagal tone
Slow exhale (longer than inhale) directly stimulates vagus
Humming/gargling vibrate vagus nerve branches
Effects measurable within minutes

articles.title: Building Your Migraine Team: Beyond the Neurologist
articles.summary: Who you need: GP, neurologist, headache specialist, therapist, PT. How to build a support team that works together. When to escalate from GP to specialist.
key_facts: GP is gateway; headache specialist understands nuances
PT addresses cervicogenic component
Therapist addresses anxiety/depression comorbidity
Multidisciplinary approach shows best outcomes
Communication between providers is critical

articles.title: Rose-Colored (or Green) Glasses: Do Tinted Lenses Actually Help?
articles.summary: FL-41 rose tint, Axon Optics, TheraSpecs. Research on precision-tinted lenses for migraine. What works, what's marketing, and what to expect.
key_facts: FL-41 tint blocks specific wavelengths that activate ipRGCs
Clinical evidence shows reduced photophobia and attack frequency
Not all tinted lenses are equal — precision tinting matters
Useful for fluorescent environments and screen work
Not a cure — part of sensory management toolkit

articles.title: The Day After: A Practical Survival Guide for the Migraine Hangover
articles.summary: Practical recovery guide for postdrome phase. What to eat, drink, do — and avoid. Tips from migraine community for faster recovery.
key_facts: Postdrome can last 24-48 hours after pain resolves
Brain is still recovering — not laziness
Gentle hydration and electrolytes aid recovery
Light movement better than complete rest
Avoid triggers that could rebound into another attack

articles.title: The Food Detective: How to Run Your Own Elimination Trial
articles.summary: How to systematically find YOUR food triggers through elimination trials — not internet myths. Cheese, chocolate, wine may not be your triggers. Individual variation is enormous.
key_facts: Individual food triggers vary enormously
Population-wide 'trigger lists' mislead most people
Systematic elimination-reintroduction is gold standard
2-week elimination, then reintroduce one food at a time
Keep detailed diary during trial

articles.title: The Ice vs. Heat Debate: What Actually Works?
articles.summary: Science behind temperature therapy. Cold constricts vessels and numbs. Heat relaxes muscles and increases flow. Finding what works for you — often combination works best.
key_facts: Cold on forehead/temples: vasoconstriction + pain gate theory
Heat on neck/shoulders: muscle relaxation, increased blood flow
Combination (cold head + warm neck) often most effective
Individual response varies significantly
Apply for 15-20 minutes max

articles.title: The McMigraine Phenomenon: What to Eat When an Attack Hits
articles.summary: McDonald's fries and Coke went viral as migraine 'cure'. Science behind it: salt, caffeine, fast carbs. Prodrome cravings are data — your brain is telling you what it needs.
key_facts: Salt, caffeine, and fast carbs address prodrome-stage deficits
Cravings during prodrome are brain signaling glucose/sodium needs
Not magic — matches what neuroscience predicts the brain needs
Small frequent meals during attack prevent glucose crash
Ginger shown to reduce nausea as effectively as some medications

articles.title: The Migraine Map: How to Choose a City That Won't Attack You
articles.summary: Data-driven framework for location choice. Factors: barometric stability, air pollution, altitude, allergens, healthcare access. Best/worst cities ranked by migraine-friendliness.
key_facts: Barometric stability matters more than absolute pressure
Air pollution directly triggers neuroinflammation
Altitude affects migraine through hypoxia
Healthcare deserts limit treatment access
Coastal cities with stable climates tend to score well

articles.title: The Side Effect Roulette: An Honest Guide to Migraine Preventive Medications
articles.summary: What preventive meds actually feel like. Topiramate brain fog, beta-blocker fatigue, amitriptyline weight gain. Every major class: what to expect, when to push through, when to switch.
key_facts: Topiramate ('Dopamax'): word-finding difficulty, tingling, weight loss, cognitive fog
Beta-blockers: fatigue, exercise intolerance, cold extremities
Amitriptyline: weight gain, dry mouth, morning grogginess
Valproate: weight gain, hair loss, teratogenic
CGRP drugs: generally well-tolerated, constipation with some
Every class has 2-3 month trial needed before judging

articles.title: Traveling with a Monster: A Migraine Survival Guide
articles.summary: Altitude changes, time zones, dehydration, stress. Travel as minefield. Practical strategies for planes, trains, and adventures abroad.
key_facts: Time zone changes disrupt circadian rhythm and cortisol
Altitude above 2500m increases migraine risk significantly
Travel stress loads multiple triggers simultaneously
Maintain medication schedule in local time
Pack double medication supply in two separate bags

articles.title: Understanding Your Triggers: What Your Patterns Are Really Telling You
articles.summary: Mi's mood stones reveal patterns. Triggers aren't simple cause-effect — they're cumulative. How to read patterns and use information for prevention.
key_facts: Triggers are cumulative (bucket theory), not single causes
Same trigger may not always cause attack — depends on total load
Pattern recognition requires consistent tracking over weeks
Prodrome symptoms can be mistaken for triggers
Mi tracks and visualizes patterns over time

articles.title: Your Migraine Emergency Kit: Never Be Caught Unprepared
articles.summary: What to pack for work, travel, and life. Practical guide to building personal migraine survival kit — medication to comfort items.
key_facts: Kit for work, car, travel bag — three locations
Medications, ice pack, sunglasses, earplugs, water, snacks
Keep medications in original containers for travel
Include comfort items: eye mask, essential oils
Update kit seasonally and after medication changes

articles.title: The 24-Hour Rule: Why That Glass of Wine Hits Different
articles.summary: Alcohol affects migraineurs differently. Not always hangover — true migraine trigger. 24-hour window. How to enjoy a drink without paying the price.
key_facts: Alcohol triggers migraine through multiple pathways: histamine, vasodilation, dehydration
Red wine most reported trigger, but individual variation huge
Trigger window extends up to 24 hours
Not the same as hangover headache
Hydration and food before/during/after reduces risk

articles.title: The Blood Sugar Rollercoaster: Glucose and Your Migraine Brain
articles.summary: Brain runs on glucose. Skipped meals, afternoon crashes, sugar spikes all trigger attacks. Steady blood sugar through regular meals is key prevention.
key_facts: Brain uses ~20% of body's glucose despite being 2% of mass
Hypoglycemia directly triggers cortical spreading depression
Skipped meals are among top 5 most common triggers
Blood sugar crashes activate stress hormones → migraine cascade
Regular small meals maintain stable glucose

articles.title: The Caffeine Paradox: Friend, Foe, or Both?
articles.summary: Coffee triggers AND relieves migraine. Caffeine half-life is 6 hours. Withdrawal triggers attacks within 12-24 hours. Finding personal balance and cutoff time is key.
key_facts: Caffeine half-life ~6 hours (varies by genetics: CYP1A2)
Withdrawal triggers attacks within 12-24 hours of last dose
200mg caffeine helps acute migraine (in some OTC meds)
Regular daily intake >200mg increases withdrawal risk
Consistent timing more important than amount

articles.title: The Cannabis Question: Relief, Trigger, or Both?
articles.summary: Some report attack abortion, others worsening. First clinical trials reporting results. What science knows and doesn't. THC vs CBD differences.
key_facts: Endocannabinoid system modulates pain, inflammation, serotonin
THC may abort attacks in some; CBD may reduce inflammation
MOH risk exists with frequent cannabis use
First RCTs underway but results limited
Varies enormously by individual, strain, route, dose
solutions: if trying: low dose, track carefully

articles.title: The Climate Migraine: How a Warming Planet Is Changing Your Brain
articles.summary: Every 10°F temperature rise = 6% more headaches. 12-year UK study (400,000 people) links air pollution and extreme heat to migraine. Climate change is a neurological crisis.
key_facts: 10°F temperature rise = 6% increase in headache ER visits
12-year UK study (n=400,000): pollution and heat linked to migraine
PM2.5 particles cross blood-brain barrier → neuroinflammation
Extreme weather events increase trigger frequency
Wildfire smoke particularly dangerous for migraineurs

articles.title: The Dehydration Equation: Why Water Isn't Enough
articles.summary: Hydration is about electrolyte balance, not just volume. You can drink 8 glasses and still be functionally dehydrated. Sodium, potassium, magnesium all matter.
key_facts: Dehydration triggers ~1/3 of migraine sufferers
Electrolyte balance matters as much as volume
Sodium and potassium drive cellular hydration
Increasing water by 1.5L cut headache hours by 21 in 2 weeks
Morning dehydration from overnight fluid loss is underrecognized

articles.title: The Doom Scroll Spiral: Social Media, Dopamine, and Your Migraine Brain
articles.summary: Not just screen light — anxiety content, dopamine loops, neck position. Mindless scrolling is an underrecognized migraine trigger combining multiple risk factors.
key_facts: Combines screen light + anxiety + dopamine depletion + neck strain
Dopamine dysregulation overlaps with migraine pathophysiology
Anxiety-triggering content activates HPA axis
Neck flexion at 45° for hours strains cervical spine
Blue light suppresses melatonin and disrupts sleep

articles.title: The Goldilocks Problem: Why Sleep Is Never 'Just Right'
articles.summary: Too little AND too much sleep trigger attacks. The maddening science of sleep and migraine. Finding your personal sweet spot.
key_facts: Both sleep deprivation AND oversleeping trigger migraine
Hypothalamus controls both sleep and migraine pathways
Consistent wake time is more important than total hours
Weekend sleep-in of >1 hour increases Monday attack risk
Sleep disorders (apnea, insomnia) increase migraine frequency

articles.title: The Screen That Stares Back: Digital Life with Migraines
articles.summary: 8 hours of screen time = 8 hours of potential triggers. What science actually says about blue light. Practical tips beyond 'just take breaks'.
key_facts: Screen flicker, blue light, and glare trigger photosensitive migraineurs
20-20-20 rule: every 20 min, look 20ft away, for 20 seconds
Dark mode may actually worsen reading in some situations
Screen brightness should match ambient lighting
Text size and contrast matter more than blue light filters

articles.title: The Trap of Relief: When Your Medicine Becomes Your Migraine
articles.summary: Medication overuse headache — the cruelest paradox. Using acute medication >10-15 days/month causes chronic daily headache. Understanding without shame. Detoxification protocols.
key_facts: Using acute meds >10-15 days/month can cause MOH
Triptans: >10 days/month threshold
NSAIDs: >15 days/month threshold
Most common cause of chronic daily headache
Detoxification is necessary but temporary worsening expected
Preventive medication should be started before/during detox

articles.title: Is This a Stroke? When Migraine Aura Becomes an Emergency
articles.summary: How to distinguish migraine aura from stroke. Numbness, speech loss, vision changes — when minutes matter. Clear guidance on when to call emergency services.
key_facts: Migraine aura: gradual onset (5-20 min), positive symptoms, serial progression
Stroke: sudden onset (seconds), negative symptoms (loss of function)
FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call
New aura pattern in someone >40 warrants urgent evaluation
When in doubt, always call emergency services
solutions: never assume 'just migraine' with new symptoms

articles.title: Little Heads, Big Pain: When Migraine Starts in Childhood
articles.summary: Abdominal migraine, cyclic vomiting, 'growing pains' that aren't. Understanding pediatric migraine and helping children who suffer in silence.
key_facts: Migraine can present as abdominal pain in children
Cyclic vomiting syndrome is a migraine variant
Children's attacks are shorter but can be more intense
Family history strongly predicts pediatric migraine
Many children go undiagnosed for years

articles.title: Migraine Hangover: The Phase No One Talks About
articles.summary: Postdrome is real. Zombie-like feeling after pain resolves. Understanding it stops self-blame for 'being lazy'. 24-48 hours of recovery is normal.
key_facts: Postdrome affects up to 80% of migraineurs
Lasts 24-48 hours after headache phase
Cognitive fog, fatigue, mood changes, food cravings
Not laziness — measurable brain changes on fMRI
Understanding postdrome reduces self-blame

articles.title: Migraine or Tension? The Question That Changes Everything
articles.summary: Throbbing vs pressing, one-sided vs both. Getting it wrong = wrong treatment. Clear diagnostic criteria and differences.
key_facts: Migraine: usually one-sided, throbbing, moderate-severe, worsened by activity
Tension: both sides, pressing/tightening, mild-moderate, not worsened by activity
Migraine has nausea, photo/phonophobia; tension type does not
40% of migraineurs are initially misdiagnosed as tension-type
Treatment differs significantly — triptans don't help tension headache

articles.title: Nine Months of Uncertainty: Migraine During Pregnancy
articles.summary: Some find relief, others new triggers. Navigating migraine with most medications off-limits. Second trimester often brings improvement due to stable estrogen.
key_facts: 60-70% of migraineurs improve during pregnancy (especially T2-T3)
Improvement linked to sustained high estrogen levels
Most migraine medications contraindicated in pregnancy
Acetaminophen generally considered safest option
Some experience first migraine or worsening during pregnancy
Postpartum estrogen crash often triggers return
solutions: discuss safe options with OB + neurologist

articles.title: The 15-Day Line: When Episodic Becomes Chronic
articles.summary: ≥15 headache days/month changes diagnosis, treatment, identity. Understanding chronic migraine and the path back. Modifiable risk factors for chronification.
key_facts: Chronic migraine: ≥15 headache days/month for ≥3 months
About 3% of episodic migraine converts to chronic each year
Modifiable risk factors: MOH, obesity, sleep disorders, depression, caffeine overuse
Botox, CGRP drugs specifically indicated for chronic migraine
Reversion to episodic is possible with proper treatment

articles.title: The Jaw You Clench at Night: TMJ, Bruxism, and the Migraine Connection
articles.summary: Bruxism activates trigeminal nerve. 70% TMJ-migraine comorbidity. Night guard reduces attacks by 40%. Jaw, teeth, and trigeminal nerve connected.
key_facts: Bruxism activates trigeminal nerve — same nerve central to migraine
70% of TMJ patients have migraine comorbidity
Night guard reduced attack frequency by ~40% in studies
Morning jaw pain/headache is hallmark sign
Stress increases nighttime clenching

articles.title: The Neck Connection: When Your Spine Talks to Your Brain
articles.summary: Cervicogenic headache vs migraine overlap. Neck problems can trigger or worsen migraine through trigeminocervical complex. Treating both may be the answer.
key_facts: Trigeminocervical complex connects neck input to migraine pathways
Cervicogenic headache mimics migraine symptoms
Neck stiffness is one of the most common prodrome symptoms
Physical therapy addressing neck can reduce migraine frequency
Posture, especially forward head posture, contributes

articles.title: The Smell of Danger: When Perfume Becomes a Weapon
articles.summary: Osmophobia affects up to 95% during attacks. Why nose becomes enemy. Perfume, chemicals, cooking smells. Protection strategies.
key_facts: Osmophobia affects up to 95% of migraineurs during attacks
Highly specific to migraine — useful diagnostic marker
Central sensitization amplifies normal smells into pain
Perfume, cleaning products, cooking most common triggers
Can persist between attacks in chronic migraine

articles.title: The Warning Signs: Reading Your Prodrome Like a Book
articles.summary: Yawning, cravings, irritability, neck stiffness — body sends signals hours before pain. Learning to read and act on prodrome.
key_facts: Prodrome begins 24-48 hours before headache
Common signs: yawning, food cravings, irritability, neck stiffness, fatigue
77% of migraineurs experience at least one prodrome symptom
Early treatment during prodrome is most effective
Prodrome symptoms often mistaken for triggers (e.g., chocolate craving)

articles.title: When the World Glitches: Understanding Migraine Aura
articles.summary: 30% of migraineurs have aura. Zigzag lines, blind spots, visual distortions. Caused by cortical spreading depression. Usually 5-60 minutes before headache.
key_facts: Affects ~30% of migraineurs
Caused by cortical spreading depression (wave of electrical activity)
Visual aura most common: zigzag lines, scotoma, flickering
Lasts 5-60 minutes, usually before headache
Can also include sensory, speech, or motor symptoms

articles.title: When Words Disappear: The Terror of Migraine Aphasia
articles.summary: Word-finding difficulty during migraine. You know the word but can't say it. Understanding language disruptions — and why they're temporary.
key_facts: Aphasia can occur as part of migraine aura
Affects word finding, sentence construction, comprehension
Caused by cortical spreading depression through language areas
Usually temporary (5-60 minutes)
Can be terrifying but doesn't indicate brain damage

articles.title: Why You Wake Up with a Migraine: The Mystery of Morning Attacks
articles.summary: Most common complaint in migraine communities. Sleep apnea, bruxism, circadian cortisol, overnight dehydration, and what your pillow can't fix.
key_facts: Morning migraine is the most common complaint in migraine communities
Multiple converging causes: sleep apnea, bruxism, cortisol trough, dehydration
4-6 AM cortisol is at lowest → reduced anti-inflammatory protection
8 hours without water = significant dehydration
Sleep apnea and migraine have 50%+ comorbidity in some studies
